1.synthesis:
Chapter 12 functions as the hymnic response to the great prophetic cycle of chapters 5–11: the song that the returning remnant of 11:16 sings on the great highway. “You will say in that day: ‘I will give thanks to you, O LORD, for though you were angry with me, your anger turned away, that you might comfort me’” (Isa 12:1). The opening line acknowledges both realities: the genuine anger that the preceding chapters catalogued (the stretched-out hand that refused to withdraw, the rod of YHWH’s fury) and the turning of that anger into comfort. The anger was real, not theatrical; the turning is equally real. This is not the pretense that judgment did not happen but the song of those who have come through it and experienced YHWH’s comfort on the other side.

The theological center of the brief song follows: “Behold, God is my salvation; I will trust, and will not be afraid; for the LORD GOD is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation” (v. 2). The words echo the Song of Moses (Exod 15:2) almost exactly: the same Lord who was the strength-and-song of the Exodus generation is again the strength-and-song of the restoration generation. The second exodus (11:16) is confirmed by the recurrence of the first exodus’s song. Verse 3 completes the section with the image that has captured Christian imagination for millennia: “With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation.” The wells of salvation are available in “that day,” and the drawing is joyful—not anxious, not rationed, not conditional.
1.historical_context: The “you will say in that day” (v. 1) formula signals that this is a song placed in the mouth of the future restored community—a gift from the prophet to the remnant, words to speak when the day comes. The anger-turned-to-comfort structure reflects the covenantal pattern of divine wrath and redemption: the same God who announced judgment through the vineyard song and the woe oracles is the one who turns the anger at the appointed time. The strength-and-song language of verse 2 is a near-quotation of Exodus 15:2 and Psalm 118:14, embedding the restoration song within the great tradition of exodus-thanksgiving. The wells-of-salvation image (v. 3) develops the water imagery that has appeared throughout these chapters: the rejected gentle waters of Shiloah (8:6) are now replaced by the wells of salvation from which the restored community draws with joy.
1.literary_structure: The Song-of-Praise section (12:1–3) has a three-verse structure: the anger-and-comfort acknowledgment (v. 1), the trust-and-salvation declaration (v. 2, with its Exodus-song echo), and the water-wells image (v. 3). The brevity of the section is part of its design: the song that encompasses the entire prophetic cycle of chapters 5–11 requires very few words because the words it uses are loaded with the weight of everything that has preceded. The anger-turned-to-comfort of verse 1 is the personal appropriation of what the entire judgment sequence has been building toward; the wells-of-salvation of verse 3 is the personal appropriation of what the great-light and Branch-from-Jesse passages have promised. The section bridges the personal thanksgiving of verses 1–3 to the communal proclamation of verses 4–6.
1.theological_application: The anger-turned-to-comfort acknowledgment (v. 1) models the mature theology of those who have survived judgment with faith intact: not denial of the anger (the woe oracles were real), not bitterness at the anger (it was just), but grateful acknowledgment that the same God who brought the judgment has also brought the comfort. Communities shaped by this passage develop a truthful theological memory that holds both the stretched-out hand and the wells of salvation without collapsing either. The trust-not-afraid declaration of verse 2 is the prophetic realization of what Isaiah called Ahaz to in 7:9—the aman that establishes. Those who have come through the judgment and discovered that YHWH’s anger did not last forever are precisely those who can now say with full conviction: I trust, and I will not be afraid.
1.christology: The wells of salvation from which the restored community draws with joy (v. 3) find their New Testament fulfillment in Christ’s conversation with the Samaritan woman: “Whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again. The water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14). On the last day of the Feast of Booths, Christ stands and cries out: “If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, ‘Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water’” (John 7:37–38). The wells of salvation that Isaiah promises are the living water that Christ himself provides: in him, the anger has turned to comfort, the judgment has become the means of the restored community’s joy, and drawing from the wells of his salvation is the daily life of those who have come through the judgment into the great light.

4.synthesis:
The song that began with personal thanksgiving now expands to communal proclamation: “And you will say in that day: ‘Give thanks to the LORD, call upon his name, make known his deeds among the peoples, proclaim that his name is exalted. Sing praises to the LORD, for he has done gloriously; let this be made known in all the earth’” (Isa 12:4–5). The movement is from the interior (I will give thanks, I will trust, v. 1–2) through the communal (you-plural will say) to the international (make known his deeds among the peoples). The proclamation of YHWH’s name and deeds is the missionary mandate embedded in the restoration song: what has happened in the return from exile and in the messianic governance of the Root of Jesse is not a private possession of the restored community but a declaration for all nations. The great deeds are to be made known in all the earth.

The chapter and the entire cycle of chapters 5–12 close with the climactic reason for the proclamation: “Shout, and sing for joy, O inhabitant of Zion, for great in your midst is the Holy One of Israel” (v. 6). The same trisagion that undid Isaiah in the throne room—holy, holy, holy (6:3)—is now the ground of the community’s joy. The Holy One who made Isaiah feel lost is now great in the midst of Zion, and the appropriate response to this presence is not the prophet’s undoing but the community’s shouting and singing. The Holy One of Israel, terrifying in the throne-room encounter, is joyful in the communal dwelling—because the burning coal has done its work, and the guilt is taken away, and the holy God has made his purified people a fit habitation.
4.historical_context: The “make known his deeds among the peoples” of verse 4 uses the same language as the great Psalm 105:1 (“give thanks to the LORD, call upon his name, make known his deeds among the peoples”), connecting the restoration song to the ongoing tradition of Israelite hymnody. The make-known mandate has historical roots in Israel’s understanding that the exodus events were designed not only for Israel’s benefit but as a testimony to YHWH’s power before the watching nations (Exod 14:4): the parting of the sea was both liberation and international proclamation. The Holy-One-of-Israel designation that closes the chapter (v. 6) is one of the signature epithets of the book of Isaiah (appearing approximately 25 times), linking the terrifying holiness of the throne-room vision to the comforting presence of the restored community’s God.
4.literary_structure: The Sing-to-the-Lord section (12:4–6) completes the song and the entire cycle of chapters 5–12. The structure is communal proclamation (vv. 4–5: give thanks, call, make known, proclaim, sing praises) → climactic reason (v. 6: great in your midst is the Holy One of Israel). The proclamation is directed outward (to the peoples, to all the earth) while the reason is directed inward (great in your midst). The outward and inward are held together: what makes the proclamation possible is the inward presence; what the inward presence requires is outward proclamation. The Holy-One-in-the-midst closing creates a perfect chiastic structure with the beginning of the prophetic cycle: Isaiah’s opening vineyard song accused Israel of failing to produce justice; the closing song declares that the Holy One is now dwelling among his restored people, the source from which justice will flow.
4.theological_application: The proclamation mandate of verse 4—make known his deeds among the peoples; proclaim that his name is exalted—is the Old Testament form of the missionary vocation that the New Testament calls the Great Commission. Communities shaped by this passage understand that the joy of restoration is inherently outward-facing: the experience of anger-turned-to-comfort, of drawing from the wells of salvation, generates the proclamatory impulse that wants all nations to know. The Holy-One-in-the-midst closing (v. 6) grounds the entire proclaiming community in the presence that makes proclamation both possible and necessary: it is because the Holy One is great in their midst that they have something to proclaim, and it is in response to his greatness that the shouting and singing arise spontaneously. The community that knows the Holy One in its midst cannot be silent.
4.christology: “Great in your midst is the Holy One of Israel” (v. 6) is fulfilled in the Incarnation: the Word who is the Holy One of Israel became flesh and “dwelt among us,” and “we have seen his glory” (John 1:14). The Holy One who was unapproachable in the throne room is now the one who is great in the midst—approachable, present, the source of joy rather than of prophetic undoing. The make-known-his-deeds mandate of verses 4–5 is taken up in its full form in the Great Commission of Matthew 28:19: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations.” The deeds to be made known are now the deeds of the Cross and Resurrection—the turning of anger into comfort accomplished in Christ’s atoning death. The shout-and-sing-for-joy of verse 6 is the prophetic anticipation of the doxology that Revelation places at the end of history: “Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God” (Rev 19:1)—the restored community of all peoples singing the song that Isaiah gave to the remnant on the great highway home.
